India · Now Open
New Store · India
Illusion
A store that does not sell.
It questions.
Gentle Monster arrives in India with its most spatially ambitious installation to date. Designed in conversation with Anish Kapoor, the space explores the threshold between seeing and perceiving — between what the eye catches and what the mind keeps.

The butterfly. The red void. The thing that is and isn't there at once.
Gentle Monster × Anish Kapoor
India · Now Open
The Installation
01 — The Central Form
The butterfly
above the void
"The visitor leaves carrying a piece of the spatial experience on their face. That is not nothing."
At the heart of the store, a butterfly image rises floor-to-ceiling behind a geometric red sculpture. The red form beneath it is not a pedestal — it is a geological event, as if the butterfly has emerged from the ground itself.

The tension between the organic wing and the crystalline base is where the installation lives. Neither is decoration. Both are questions.
— Anish Kapoor on the central installation
02 — The Space
The threshold
is the work
"India demands that you read it first. A store that arrives here and pretends it is arriving anywhere — that is a failure of intelligence."
From this angle you see the store's spatial logic: the red void sculpture anchors the floor, the butterfly installation towers behind it, and the eyewear shelves — white, curved, almost biological — wrap the walls at a distance, as if observing.

The black form on the left wall is not accidental. It is Kapoor's shadow — a reminder that every light source has a negative.
— Anish Kapoor
Inside the Store
The Collection Wall
Curved organic shelving wraps the store — each frame displayed as a singular object against white.
The Threshold Arch
A fractured marble arch — part ruin, part portal — frames the passage between spaces. Red textile spills from the break like something alive.
The Display Rooms
Individually lit frames line the walls in grid formation — each piece in its own framed case, presented like specimens, or small paintings.
"A question. They become a question. And in India — a country that has been producing questions for five thousand years — that feels exactly right."
— Anish Kapoor · Gentle Monster India
The Living Room
Soft forms in a hard space
The seating — low, sculptural, almost melted — creates pockets of rest inside the installation. You are invited to sit with the work, not just move past it.
The Second Room
Stone, textile, and a lotus
The fractured arch installation features lotus plants growing from the red textile floor — a deliberately Indian reference, handled without sentimentality. Life inside the rupture.
The Illusion
Red. The colour
of seeing.
The red in this store is not decorative. It is Kapoor's red — the deep, almost physical red that recurs across his sculptures and pigment works. It is present in the floor sculpture, in the textile, in the subtle wash of the butterfly image.

In Kapoor's language, red is not warm. It is a proposition. It asks: what are you looking at, and why does it hold you?

Gold was never the decoration. It was always the question.
The Store
Come and
see it yourself
"I didn't set out to make work about India. But it's there."
From the outside, the store announces itself with restraint — the GENTLE MONSTER signage, the butterfly visible through the glass, the red sculpture glimpsed beyond it. The window is already the beginning of the experience.

Once inside, the space does the rest.
